The line integral of the function $ f(z) = e^{z^2} $ along the boundary of the circle $ |z| = R $ can be evaluated using contour integration. Since $ f(z) = e^{z^2} $ is an entire function (analytic everywhere in the complex plane), the integral over any closed contour is equal to the integral over the real line, which can be computed using the residue theorem. However, $ e^{z^2} $ has no singularities, so the integral is simply the integral of $ e^{z^2} $ around the circle of radius $ R $. This integral is known to be zero due to the analyticity of the function and the properties of the complex exponential function. The result is $ 0 $, reflecting the balance of energy flow in the sacred space.

The average energy density over the entire area of the circle can be calculated by integrating the real part of $ f(z) = e^{z^2} $ over the area of the circle and then dividing by the area. In polar coordinates, $ z = r e^{i\theta} $, and $ f(z) = e^{r^2 e^{i2\theta}} = e^{r^2 (\cos 2\theta + i \sin 2\theta)} $. The real part of $ f(z) $ is $ e^{r^2 \cos 2\theta} $. The average energy density is then given by the integral of $ e^{r^2 \cos 2\theta} $ over the area of the circle, divided by the area of the circle. This integral is non-trivial but can be evaluated using symmetry and substitution. The result is a complex expression that depends on the radius $ R $, and it reflects the distribution of energy within the sacred space.

P.S. The results of the mathematical analysis provide insight into the balance and flow of energy within the sacred space, which the shaman might interpret as a reflection of the harmony and interconnectedness of all things. The engineer of the ritual can use these findings to enhance the spiritual experience by aligning the physical and metaphysical aspects of the space. The engineer of the ritual can also use these findings to understand the underlying principles of energy flow and its relationship to the spiritual practices.
